---
title: "KV Cache Explained"
---

**KV cache** (Key-Value cache) is an inference-time optimization for transformer-based LLMs that stores the **Key (K)** and **Value (V)** projections from previous tokens, so they don't have to be recomputed at every generation step.

To understand why it matters, let's start with what happens during autoregressive generation.

## The Problem: Naive Generation is Wasteful

When an LLM generates text, it produces one token at a time. Each new token requires a full forward pass through the transformer. Inside each attention layer, every token needs three vectors: Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V).

Without caching, here's what happens:

```
Step 1: Generate token after "The"
        Compute K, V for: [The]
        
Step 2: Generate token after "The cat"
        Compute K, V for: [The, cat]    ← recomputes "The" again!
        
Step 3: Generate token after "The cat sat"
        Compute K, V for: [The, cat, sat]  ← recomputes "The", "cat" again!
        
Step N: ...
        Compute K, V for: [tok_1, tok_2, ..., tok_N]  ← O(N²) total work
```

Notice the wasted computation: K and V for "The" never change once it's been processed, but we keep recomputing them. For a 10,000-token context, this is enormous.

## The Solution: Cache K and V

The insight: in causal (decoder-only) attention, a past token's K and V vectors are **deterministic functions of that token and its position** — they don't depend on future tokens. So we can compute them once and store them.

```
                    KV CACHE (grows as we generate)
                    ┌─────────────────────────────┐
Layer L:            │  K₁  K₂  K₃  ...  Kₙ        │
                    │  V₁  V₂  V₃  ...  Vₙ        │
                    └──────────────▲──────────────┘
                                   │
                                   │ append new (Kₙ₊₁, Vₙ₊₁)
                                   │
                       ┌───────────┴───────────┐
                       │ New token at step n+1 │
                       │  → compute Q, K, V    │
                       │    only for THIS token│
                       └───────────────────────┘
```

So at generation step *n+1*, we only compute Q, K, V for the **single new token**, then attend over the cached K and V from all previous tokens. This turns each generation step from O(N) work into O(1) work for the K/V projections (attention itself is still O(N) for the dot products, but that's unavoidable).

## Why "K and V" but not Q?

This often confuses people. Here's the intuition:

```
Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax( Q · Kᵀ / √d ) · V
                     ╰──────┬──────╯
                       attention scores

  Q  ──► current token asking: "what do I attend to?"
  K  ──► past tokens advertising: "here's what I am"
  V  ──► past tokens offering: "here's my content"
```

Only the **current** token needs a Q (it's the one doing the asking). All past tokens need their K and V available, but they don't need Qs anymore — they've already done their asking in earlier steps.

## The Trade-off: Memory Cost

KV cache is fast but **memory-hungry**. Cache size scales as:

```
cache_size ≈ 2 × n_layers × n_heads × head_dim × seq_len × batch × precision_bytes
              │                                   │
              └─ 2 because K and V                └─ this is what blows up
```

For a 70B-parameter model with a 100k-token context, the KV cache can be **tens of gigabytes** — often larger than the model weights themselves. This is why long-context inference is expensive and why techniques like **Multi-Query Attention (MQA)**, **Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)**, and **PagedAttention** (vLLM) exist — they're all about shrinking or managing the KV cache.

## Why This Matters for Agentic AI

Agentic workflows hit the KV cache hard because they involve:

- **Long contexts** (tool definitions, system prompts, conversation history, retrieved documents)
- **Many turns** (each tool call adds tokens to the context)
- **Repeated prefixes** (the same system prompt across many calls)

Modern inference engines exploit this with **prefix caching** — if two requests share a prefix (e.g., the same system prompt), the K and V for that prefix are computed once and reused across requests. Anthropic's API exposes this as **prompt caching**, which can cut costs and latency dramatically for agentic loops where you keep hitting the model with the same lengthy preamble.

```
Without prompt caching:           With prompt caching:
┌─────────────────┐               ┌─────────────────┐
│ System (5k tok) │ recompute     │ System (5k tok) │ ◄── cached KV
├─────────────────┤   every       ├─────────────────┤      (reused)
│ Tools  (3k tok) │   call        │ Tools  (3k tok) │ ◄── cached KV
├─────────────────┤               ├─────────────────┤
│ Turn 1 input    │               │ Turn 1 input    │ ◄── only this is new
└─────────────────┘               └─────────────────┘
   8k tok work                        ~1k tok work
```

So in short: **KV cache turns inference from quadratic to linear in sequence length**, at the cost of memory — and it's the single most important optimization underlying modern LLM serving, especially for agents.
